documents.title: Ghostty Docs
documents.description:
Ghostty is a fast, feature-rich, and cross-platform terminal emulator
that uses platform-native UI and GPU acceleration.
documents.summary: This document introduces Ghostty, a terminal emulator characterized by its speed, extensive features, and cross-platform compatibility achieved through native UI and GPU acceleration.

documents.title: Download Ghostty
documents.description: Ghostty is a fast, feature-rich, and cross-platform terminal emulator that uses platform-native UI and GPU acceleration.
documents.summary: This document outlines the installation methods for Ghostty version 1.3.0 on both macOS and Linux operating systems.

documents.title: Ghostty
documents.description: Ghostty is a fast, feature-rich, and cross-platform terminal emulator that uses platform-native UI and GPU acceleration.
documents.summary: Ghostty is a cross-platform terminal emulator emphasizing speed, rich features, and performance through native UI and GPU acceleration.

documents.title: Configuration
documents.description:
Ghostty supports hundreds of configuration options to make
it look and behave exactly how you want.
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure the Ghostty terminal emulator using its text-based configuration file, detailing file locations, supported syntax, and methods for splitting configuration across multiple files.

documents.title: Financial Support
documents.description:
Ghostty is fiscally sponsored by Hack Club (501(c)(3)).
All donations are tax-deductible in the United States.
documents.summary: This document explains how the Ghostty project is supported financially through donations via its fiscal sponsor, Hack Club, detailing how funds are allocated and the benefits of its non-profit structure.

documents.title: About Ghostty
documents.summary: Ghostty is a terminal emulator designed to be fast, feature-rich, and native, uniquely achieving competitiveness across all three areas by utilizing a shared core library called libghostty for cross-platform emulation.

documents.title: Build from Source - Install
documents.description:
Build Ghostty yourself from source code. This is only recommended for
people who don't have access to binaries or packages, or for developers
who want to contribute to Ghostty.
documents.summary: This document provides instructions for compiling the Ghostty terminal emulator from its source code, detailing prerequisites like installing the correct Zig compiler version and platform-specific dependencies, followed by the build and installation steps.

documents.title: Color Theme - Features
documents.description:
Ghostty supports full customization of the color theme,
ships with hundreds of built-in themes, supports custom themes
for light and dark mode, and more.
documents.summary: This document explains how to customize the color theme in Ghostty using built-in themes, specifying light and dark modes, and authoring custom theme files.

documents.title: Keybindings - Configuration
documents.description: Associate a keypress with a specific action.
documents.summary: This document explains how to define custom keyboard shortcuts in Ghostty using the `keybind` configuration option, detailing the syntax for triggers, modifiers, prefixes, and available actions.

documents.title: Terminal API (VT)
documents.description: APIs for developers writing applications that run in Ghostty.
documents.summary: This document provides an overview of how Ghostty supports control sequences (VT sequences or escape codes) used by terminal programs to interact with the terminal.

documents.title: Binaries and Packages - Install
documents.description:
Install Ghostty without building it manually by downloading
and running a prebuilt binary or package.
documents.summary: This document outlines the various methods available for installing the Ghostty terminal emulator using prebuilt binaries and packages across different operating systems, noting which sources are official and which are community-maintained.

documents.title: 1.3.0 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.3.0, released on March 9, 2026.
documents.summary: This document details the new features, improvements, and bug fixes included in the Ghostty 1.3.0 release, highlighting major additions like scrollback search, native scrollbars, and enhanced shell prompt interaction.

documents.title: Option Reference - Configuration
documents.description: Reference of all Ghostty configuration options.
documents.summary: This document serves as a reference detailing various configuration options for the Ghostty terminal emulator, focusing primarily on settings related to font selection, styling, and GUI language.

documents.title: Packaging Ghostty - Install
documents.description: For people who want to build and package Ghostty for their platform.
documents.summary: This document directs users interested in building and distributing the Ghostty software to the official packaging guide located within the source tree.

documents.title: AppleScript (macOS) - Features
documents.description:
Automate Ghostty on macOS with AppleScript. This guide covers
the object model, available commands, and practical examples.
documents.summary: This document provides a comprehensive guide to automating the Ghostty terminal emulator on macOS using AppleScript, detailing the object model, available commands for window and terminal manipulation, and configuration options.

documents.title: Prerelease Builds - Install
documents.description:
Help test the latest features and get the most recent bug fixes
by running prerelease builds of Ghostty.
documents.summary: This document explains how users can install and use prerelease (tip) builds of the Ghostty terminal emulator across various operating systems to test new features and report bugs.

documents.title: Shell Integration - Features
documents.description:
Some Ghostty features require integrating with your shell. Ghostty
can automatically inject shell integration for bash, zsh, fish, and
elvish.
documents.summary: This document explains how Ghostty automatically injects shell integration features for supported shells like bash, zsh, fish, and elvish, and provides instructions for manual sourcing if automatic injection fails or when switching shells.

documents.title: Action Reference - Keybindings
documents.description: Reference of all Ghostty keybinding actions.
documents.summary: This document serves as a reference listing and describing all available keybinding actions within the Ghostty terminal emulator.

documents.title: Features
documents.description: The features Ghostty supports and associated documentation.
documents.summary: This document details the extensive features supported by the Ghostty terminal emulator, categorized for both end-users and application developers, highlighting its cross-platform capability and commitment to platform-native behavior.

documents.title: Reference - Terminal API (VT)
documents.description: A reference of all VT sequences supported by Ghostty.
documents.summary: This document serves as a reference listing the various VT sequences supported by the Ghostty terminal emulator, organized by sequence type.

documents.title: Trigger Sequences - Keybindings
documents.description: A trigger that requires a sequence of multiple keypress events.
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure keybindings in Ghostty that require a specific sequence of keypress events to activate an action, using the '>' separator.

documents.title: Control Sequences - Concepts
documents.description: Control sequences are a way for programs to interact with the terminal.
documents.summary: This document explains terminal control sequences, which programs use to send commands like cursor movement and color changes to the terminal, detailing the various formats such as C0, CSI, OSC, DCS, and APC sequences.

documents.title: 1.1.1 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.1.1, released on February 13, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the fixes, improvements, and new features introduced in Ghostty version 1.1.1, primarily focusing on correcting regressions from the 1.1.0 release, including improvements to Server-Side Decorations (SSD) on X11 and enhanced IME support across platforms.

documents.title: Release Notes - Install
documents.description:
List of all published version of Ghostty and their accompanying
release notes.
documents.summary: This document provides an index listing all published versions of the Ghostty software along with links to their corresponding release notes.

documents.title: Linux
documents.description:
Ghostty works great on Linux, with support for both Wayland 
and X11 and packages across a variety of distributions and
formats.
documents.summary: This document provides Linux-specific instructions and prerequisites for installing and running the Ghostty terminal emulator, focusing on installation methods and minimum required GTK and Adwaita versions.

documents.title: 1.2.3 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.2.3, released on October 23, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the release notes for Ghostty version 1.2.3, highlighting critical fixes for deadlocks, titlebar styling issues on macOS, and improvements to font rendering, especially for icon glyphs.

documents.title: External Protocols - Terminal API (VT)
documents.description: A list of external protocols that Ghostty supports.
documents.summary: This document lists the external terminal emulator protocols that Ghostty supports, such as Hyperlinks and Kitty Color Protocol.

documents.title: 1.2.2 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.2.2, released on October 8, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the release notes for Ghostty version 1.2.2, primarily addressing a critical memory leak regression from the previous version and a minor font rendering fix on macOS.

documents.title: Backspace (BS) - Control
documents.description: Move the cursor backward one position.
documents.summary: This document explains the function of the Backspace (BS) control character, which moves the cursor backward by one position in a terminal interface.

documents.title: Tab (TAB) - Control
documents.description: Move the cursor right to the next tab stop.
documents.summary: This document describes the control sequence used to move the terminal cursor to the next tab stop position.

documents.title: 1.2.0 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.2.0, released on September 15, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the major features and improvements introduced in Ghostty version 1.2.0, including a new command palette, configuration options for quick terminal sizing, updated icons for macOS and Linux, and rendering backend rework for better feature parity.

documents.title: 1.0.1 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.0.1, released on December 31, 2024.
documents.summary: These release notes detail version 1.0.1 of Ghostty, highlighting security fixes, configuration improvements like default file creation and better settings opening logic, and new feature support for bitmap fonts and shorthand hex color values.

documents.title: Bell (BEL) - Control
documents.description: Raise the attention of the user.
documents.summary: This document explains the purpose of the Bell (BEL) control character (ASCII 0x07), which is used to alert the user, and notes its historical usage and modern implementation possibilities.

documents.title: Index (IND) - ESC
documents.description: Move the cursor down one cell, scrolling if necessary.
documents.summary: This document details the behavior of the DEC Index (IND) control sequence, which moves the cursor down one cell, potentially causing scrolling based on cursor position relative to the screen margins and defined scroll regions.

documents.title: 1.1.2 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.1.2, released on February 13, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the release notes for Ghostty version 1.1.2, which serves as a hotfix for a critical macOS regression affecting control-modified keys in Kitty Keyboard protocol applications.

documents.title: Screen Alignment Test (DECALN) - ESC
documents.description: Reset margins, move cursor to the top left, and fill the screen with `E`.
documents.summary: This document details the behavior and usage of the DEC ALN (Declare Alignment) control sequence, which resets screen margins, moves the cursor, and fills the screen with the character 'E'.

documents.title: Restore Cursor (DECRC) - ESC
documents.description: Restore the cursor-related state saved via Save Cursor (DECSC).
documents.summary: This document describes the ANSI escape sequence used to restore the cursor state that was previously saved using the Save Cursor (DECSC) command.

documents.title: Save Cursor (DECSC) - ESC
documents.description:
Save various cursor-related state that can be restored with
Restore Cursor (DECRC).
documents.summary: This document explains the functionality of the Save Cursor (DECSC) control sequence, detailing which attributes are saved and how it interacts with cursor state across primary and alternate screens.

documents.title: Set Cursor Style (DECSCUSR) - CSI
documents.description: Set the cursor style.
documents.summary: This document details the ANSI escape sequence used to set the cursor style in a terminal, specifying the meaning of the optional parameter 'n'. It provides a mapping between integer values of 'n' and the resulting cursor appearance.

documents.title: Carriage Return (CR) - Control
documents.description: Move the cursor to the leftmost column.
documents.summary: This document explains the behavior of the Carriage Return (CR, 0x0D) control sequence on the cursor position, detailing how it interacts with the pending wrap state and the concept of left margin settings.

documents.title: 1.1.3 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.1.3, released on March 24, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the release notes for Ghostty version 1.1.3, which focuses primarily on fixing critical compatibility issues with Gnome 48 and GTK 4.18, along with various platform-specific bug fixes.

documents.title: 1.1.0 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.1.0, released on January 30, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the features, bug fixes, and improvements included in the Ghostty 1.1.0 release, highlighting changes to window decoration support on Linux, enhanced IME reliability, new keybinding capabilities, and alpha blending improvements on macOS.

documents.title: Reset to Initial State (RIS) - ESC
documents.description: Reset the terminal to its initial state.
documents.summary: This document describes the Full Reset (RIS) sequence for terminal emulation, which reverts the terminal to its initial, default operational state.

documents.title: Keypad Application Mode (DECKPAM) - ESC
documents.description: Set the numeric keypad to application mode.
documents.summary: This document outlines the escape sequence used to set the numeric keypad to application mode in a terminal environment.

documents.title: 1.2.1 - Release Notes
documents.description: Release notes for Ghostty 1.2.1, released on October 6, 2025.
documents.summary: This document details the release notes for Ghostty version 1.2.1, focusing on fixes for issues introduced in the previous version, improvements to font rendering, automatic PATH configuration via shell integration, and new settings for mouse scroll multipliers.

documents.title: Cursor Next Line (CNL) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor down `n` cells and to the beginning of the line.
documents.summary: This document explains the ANSI escape sequence required to move the cursor down a specified number of cells and then return it to the beginning of the line.

documents.title: Linefeed (LF) - Control
documents.description: Move the cursor down one line, scrolling if necessary.
documents.summary: This document describes the action to move the terminal cursor down by one line, including necessary scrolling.

documents.title: Cursor Down (CUD) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor down `n` cells.
documents.summary: This document details the ANSI escape sequence used to move the terminal cursor down a specified number of cells, including parameter constraints and behavior related to scrolling margins.

documents.title: Reverse Index (RI) - ESC
documents.description: Move the cursor up one cell, scrolling if necessary.
documents.summary: This document details the behavior of the Reverse Index (RI) escape sequence, which moves the cursor up one cell and potentially triggers scrolling based on cursor position relative to screen margins and defined scroll regions.

documents.title: Cursor Backward (CUB) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor left `n` cells.
documents.summary: This document details the Control Sequence Introducer (CSI) sequence CU/B (Cursor Backward) which moves the terminal cursor left by a specified number of cells, explaining the underlying escape sequence and its behavior variations based on terminal mode settings like wraparound and reverse wrap.

documents.title: Cursor Forward (CUF) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor `n` cells right.
documents.summary: This document details the Control Sequence Introducer (CSI) command used to move the cursor a specified number of cells to the right, explaining its syntax, parameter handling, boundary conditions, and behavior regarding terminal state.

documents.title: Cursor Up (CUU) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor `n` cells up.
documents.summary: This document details the sequence and behavior for moving the terminal cursor a specified number of cells up, including rules for handling parameters and margin constraints.

documents.title: Cursor Position (CUP) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor to row `y` and column `x`.
documents.summary: This document details the Control Sequence Introducer (CSI) command used to move the terminal cursor to a specific row and column, explaining parameter handling and behavior under different origin modes.

documents.title: Erase in Display (ED) - CSI
documents.description: Erase display contents with behavior depending on the command `n`.
documents.summary: This document describes the Control Sequence Introducer (CSI) command used to erase display contents, detailing how the parameter 'n' (0, 1, 2, or 3) dictates whether to erase below, above, the entire screen, or just the scrollback region.

documents.title: Cursor Horizontal Tabulation (CHT) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor right `n` tabs.
documents.summary: This document describes the CSI sequence used to move the cursor right to the next tab stop, detailing the required parameters and behavior regarding screen boundaries and tabstop settings.

documents.title: Keypad Numeric Mode (DECKPNM) - ESC
documents.description: Set the numeric keypad to numeric mode.
documents.summary: This document specifies the control sequences used to set the numeric keypad to numeric mode within a terminal environment.

documents.title: Cursor Preceding Line (CPL) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor up `n` cells and to the beginning of the line.
documents.summary: This document details the control sequence for moving the terminal cursor up 'n' lines to the start of the respective lines, known as Cursor Previous Line (CPL). It specifies the required byte sequence and parameter handling for the 'n' parameter.

documents.title: Delete Line (DL) - CSI
documents.description:
Deletes `n` lines at the current cursor position and shifts existing
lines up.
documents.summary: This document describes the Delete Line (DL) control sequence, which removes a specified number of lines at the cursor position and shifts subsequent lines upward, detailing its parameters, behavior related to scroll regions, and handling of multi-cell characters.

documents.title: Erase in Line (EL) - CSI
documents.description: Erase line contents with behavior depending on the command `n`.
documents.summary: This document details the behavior of the ANSI Escape Sequence CSI n K (EL), which is used to erase content on the current line based on the parameter n (0 for right, 1 for left, 2 for the entire line).

documents.title: Erase Character (ECH) - CSI
documents.description:
Blank `n` cells beginning with and including the cursor through to
the right of the cursor.
documents.summary: This document explains the behavior of the Erase in Character (ECH) Control Sequence, specifically how it blanks cells to the right of the cursor, handles parameters, SGR state, multi-cell characters, and protected attributes.

documents.title: Vertical Position Relative (VPR) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor to a specific row relative to the current position.
documents.summary: This document describes the VT sequence VPR, which moves the cursor vertically by a specified relative amount based on the current row position.

documents.title: Scroll Down (SD) - CSI
documents.description:
Insert `n` lines at the top of the scroll region and
shift existing lines down.
documents.summary: This document details the terminal control sequence 'SD' (Scroll Down), which inserts 'n' lines at the top of the current scroll region, shifting existing content down while preserving the cursor position.

documents.title: Delete Character (DCH) - CSI
documents.description:
Delete `n` characters at the current cursor position and shift existing
cell contents left.
documents.summary: This document details the ANSI escape sequence used to delete $n$ characters at the current cursor position, shifting subsequent content left and outlining behavior regarding scroll regions and multi-cell characters.

documents.title: Scroll Up (SU) - CSI
documents.description:
Remove `n` lines from the top of the scroll region and shift existing
lines up.
documents.summary: This document explains the usage of the terminal control sequence (CSI) 'SU' (Scroll Up), which removes a specified number of lines from the top of the scroll region and shifts the remaining lines up.

documents.title: Insert Line (IL) - CSI
documents.description: Insert blank lines at the current cursor position.
documents.summary: This document describes the ANSI escape sequence 'Insert Line' (IL), which inserts specified blank lines at the current cursor position, shifting existing content down, and details its behavior concerning cursor position relative to scroll regions and margins.

documents.title: Cursor Backward Tabulation (CBT) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor `n` tabs left.
documents.summary: This document details the Control Sequence (CSI) command for moving the cursor backward to the preceding tab stop or the leftmost valid column, known as Cursor Backward Tabulate (CBT). It explains the sequence of bytes required and the rules governing cursor movement relative to tab stops and margins.

documents.title: Device Status Report (DSR) - CSI
documents.description: Request information from the terminal.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use Control Sequence Introducers (CSI) sequences, specifically Device Status Reports (DSR), to request status information like operating status or cursor position from a terminal.

documents.title: Tab Clear (TBC) - CSI
documents.description: Clear one or all tab stops.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the ANSI escape sequence CSI ? n g to clear one or all terminal tab stops, specifying the behavior based on the parameter 'n' being 0 or 3.

documents.title: Vertical Position Absolute (VPA) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor to a specific row.
documents.summary: This document describes the VT escape sequence VPA (Vertical Position Absolute) used to move the cursor to a specific row in a terminal interface.

documents.title: Set Left and Right Margins (DECSLRM) - CSI
documents.description: Set the left and right margins.
documents.summary: This document explains the control sequence used to set the left and right margins (scroll region) in a terminal environment, detailing required prerequisite modes, parameter behavior, and conflicts.

documents.title: Repeat (REP) - CSI
documents.description: Repeat the previously printed character `n` times.
documents.summary: This document explains the Repeat Previous Character (REP) control sequence, detailing its structure, parameter constraints, and behavior regarding character repetition in terminal environments.

documents.title: Change Window Icon (OSC 1) - OSC
documents.description: Change the name of the window icon.
documents.summary: This document outlines the sequence of control characters (OSC 1) used to attempt to change the name of the window icon in terminal emulators, noting its limited and often unsupported implementation.

documents.title: Insert Character (ICH) - CSI
documents.description: Insert blank characters at the current cursor position.
documents.summary: This document details the use of the Insert Character (ICH) Control Sequence, represented by CSI 2@ or ESC [ n @, which inserts a specified number of blank characters at the current cursor position, shifting existing content to the right.

documents.title: Horizontal Position Relative (HPR) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor to a specific column relative to the current position.
documents.summary: This document explains the function and control sequence for the Horizontal Position Reply (HPR) terminal command, which moves the cursor horizontally relative to its current position.

documents.title: Change Window Icon and Title (OSC 0) - OSC
documents.description: Simultaneously change the window icon name and title.
documents.summary: This document details the byte sequences for several control characters and explains that the specific sequence 0x1B 0x5C maps to an alias for the Change Window Title operating system command (OSC 2) in the Ghostty terminal emulator.

documents.title: Change Working Directory (OSC 7) - OSC
documents.description: Change the current working directory of the terminal.
documents.summary: This document describes the control sequence used by terminal emulators to signal a change in the current working directory to an integrated shell, often using a specific Operating System Command (OSC) sequence.

documents.title: Query or Change Palette Colors (OSC 4) - OSC
documents.description: Query or change colors in the palette based on their indices.
documents.summary: This document explains the use of the operating system command (OSC) sequence 4 for querying and modifying colors within the terminal's palette based on an index and a new color value.

documents.title: Query or Change Special Colors (OSC 5) - OSC
documents.description: Query or change a special color based on its index.
documents.summary: This document explains the usage of an escape sequence for querying or changing the five special terminal colors based on an index and a control character.

documents.title: Horizontal Position Absolute (HPA) - CSI
documents.description: Move the cursor to a specific column.
documents.summary: This document explains the control sequence used to move the cursor to a specific column, which utilizes the Cursor Position (CUP) sequence but manipulates the row parameter based on context.

documents.title: Change Window Title (OSC 2) - OSC
documents.description: Change the name of the window title.
documents.summary: This document outlines the specific sequence of control codes (OSC 2) required to change the window title in a terminal environment.

documents.title: Shift-Escape Behavior (XTSHIFTESCAPE) - CSI
documents.description:
Configure whether mouse reports are allowed to capture
the `shift` modifier.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the XTSHIFTESCAPE sequence to configure whether the terminal allows mouse reports to capture the shift modifier, which affects text selection versus program detection of shift-clicks.

documents.title: Set Top and Bottom Margins (DECSTBM) - CSI
documents.description: Set the top and bottom margins, otherwise known as the scroll region.
documents.summary: This document explains the ANSI/VT escape sequence DECSTBM ([t;br) used to set the top and bottom margins, defining the terminal's scroll region, and details the required parameters and resulting cursor behavior.

documents.title: Change Pointer Shape (OSC 22) - OSC
documents.description: Change the pointer shape.
documents.summary: This document details the use of Operating System Command (OSC) sequence 22 to change the terminal pointer shape and discusses the lack of standardization across different terminal emulators.

documents.title: ConEmu Extensions (OSC 9;n) - OSC
documents.description: Various extensions pioneered by ConEmu.
documents.summary: This document details how the Ghostty terminal emulator handles and resolves conflicts with custom protocols, specifically those pioneered by ConEmu using OSC 9, by attempting to parse sub-IDs to differentiate sequences.

documents.title: Query or Change Dynamic Colors (OSC 10–19) - OSC
documents.description: Query or change dynamic colors based on their indices.
documents.summary: This document describes how to query or change dynamic terminal colors based on a specific sequence structure involving an index 'n' (10-19) and color specifications 'c'.

documents.title: Query or Change Clipboard Data (OSC 52) - OSC
documents.description: Query or change data on clipboards.
documents.summary: This document explains the format and behavior of OSC 52 sequences used to query or change data associated with various system clipboards.

documents.title: Reset Special Colors (OSC 105) - OSC
documents.description: Reset special colors.
documents.summary: This document details the structure and function of OSC sequence 105, which is used to reset special terminal colors, optionally specifying which color indexes to affect.

documents.title: Show Desktop Notification (OSC 9) - OSC
documents.description: Show a desktop notification.
documents.summary: This document details the use of OSC 9 sequences for displaying desktop notifications, including a special consideration for avoiding conflicts with ConEmu extensions when setting the notification title.

documents.title: Reset Palette Colors (OSC 104) - OSC
documents.description: Reset colors in the palette.
documents.summary: This document explains the sequence of escape codes used to reset colors within a terminal palette, detailing how to target specific color indices or reset the entire palette.

documents.title: Reset Dynamic Colors (OSC 110–119) - OSC
documents.description: Reset dynamic colors based on their indices.
documents.summary: This document explains the use of the OSC sequence 11x to reset specific dynamic colors based on the numerical index provided.

documents.title: Terminfo - Help
documents.description:
Terminfo is used to tell software about the terminal's capabilities.
Ghostty ships with its own terminfo entry to advertise its features.
documents.summary: This document explains how Ghostty uses the terminfo format, specifically setting the TERM variable to 'xterm-ghostty', and provides solutions for compatibility issues encountered when using sudo or connecting over SSH to machines without the necessary terminfo entry.

documents.title: Colors - Concepts
documents.description: All sorts of information regarding colors in terminal emulation.
documents.summary: This document explains the color capabilities of Ghostty, detailing its 256-color palette, special colors, dynamic colors, and the various color specification formats it supports for defining colors.

documents.title: Screen - Concepts
documents.description: TODO
documents.summary: This document provides reference material and conceptual explanations for the Ghostty terminal emulator, specifically detailing aspects of its Virtual Terminal (VT) implementation.

documents.title: Systemd and D-Bus - Linux
documents.description:
Ghostty comes with built-in support for D-Bus activation and
`systemd` services to launch on demand and create new windows
instantly.
documents.summary: This document explains how Ghostty leverages systemd and D-Bus integration to enable on-demand launching, instantaneous window creation, and centralized logging, detailing configuration steps and underlying mechanisms.

documents.title: Cursor - Concepts
documents.description:
The cursor is the row and column where the next
character will be printed or location-sensitive control
sequence will be executed.
documents.summary: This document explains the concept of the terminal cursor, defining its location, its role in character printing and control sequences, and introducing the related concept of the pending wrap state.

documents.title: GTK Single Instance - Help
documents.description:
GTK single instance mode is a feature that ensures only one instance of the
application is running at a time.
documents.summary: This document explains how Ghostty utilizes GTK single instance mode by default when not launched from a command-line interface, and details the heuristics used for detection and how users can force this behavior on or off.

documents.title: macOS Tiling Window Managers - Help
documents.description:
Ghostty tabs may render as separate windows in macOS tiling
window managers such as Yabai or Aerospace.
documents.summary: This document explains why Ghostty tabs may appear as separate windows when using macOS tiling window managers like Yabai or Aerospace, noting it's a limitation of native macOS tabs and API integration.

documents.title: Help
documents.description:
How to seek help, a list of common issues and solutions,
and how to report a bug or request a feature.
documents.summary: This document explains how users can seek help, find solutions to common issues encountered with Ghostty, and details the correct procedure for reporting bugs or requesting new features.

documents.title: Synchronized Output - Help
documents.description:
Command-line programs that update large swaths of the visible screen might
show screen tearing on fast terminals like Ghostty, when synchronized
output is not enabled.
documents.summary: This document explains that screen tearing or flickering in the Ghostty terminal emulator, often seen with rapidly updating command-line programs, is typically due to the application not synchronizing its output correctly, and advises developers on implementing the Synchronized Output protocol or optimizing cell updates.

documents.title: macOS Login Shells - Help
documents.description:
In other Unix systems terminal emulators are generally started
using non-login shells. Due to various reasons, macOS defaults
all terminals as login shells.
documents.summary: This document explains why macOS defaults terminal shells to be login shells, contrasting this behavior with other Unix systems and detailing the implications for shell configuration files like .zprofile and .zshrc.

documents.title: GTK OpenGL Context - Help
documents.description:
In some situations, Ghostty may fail to start with an error about being
"unable to acquire an OpenGL context". This page adds more details about
this error and how to resolve it.
documents.summary: This document explains troubleshooting steps for the Ghostty error where it fails to start due to being unable to acquire an OpenGL context from GTK, which is typically caused by environment or driver issues.
